section.title: Children, Parents, and Servants
section.sectionRationale: Paul continues the household code begun at 5:21, moving from husbands and wives to the other two pairs of the ancient household: children and parents, and slaves and masters. As with marriage, the point is not merely social order but the reordering of every relationship around the lordship of Christ. Children obey in the Lord, fathers raise their children in the discipline of the Lord, slaves serve as servants of Christ, and masters remember that they too have a Master in heaven who shows no favoritism. The gospel reaches into the most ordinary and the most fraught of human bonds.
section.thesis: In the Lord, children are to obey and honor their parents and fathers to nurture rather than provoke, while slaves are to serve wholeheartedly as servants of Christ and masters to treat them justly, because both slave and free stand before the same heavenly Master who rewards each and shows no favoritism.
section.frameNote: Two movements: children and parents, obedience honored by God's own promise and fathers charged to raise their children in the Lord; and slaves and masters, wholehearted service rendered to Christ and authority tempered by the knowledge of a common Master in heaven who has no favorites.

units.label: Children and parents
units.note: obey in the Lord, raise in the Lord

verses.text: Children, {{hupakouo|obey}} your parents in the Lord, for this is right.
"Honor your father and mother" (which is the first {{entole|commandment}} with a promise),
"that it may go well with you and that you may have a long life on the earth."
Fathers, do not provoke your children to wrath; instead, bring them up in the {{paideia|discipline}} and instruction of the Lord.

units.label: Slaves and masters
units.note: one Master in heaven, no favoritism

verses.text: {{doulos|Slaves}}, obey your earthly masters with respect and fear and sincerity of heart, just as you would obey Christ.
And do this not only to please them while they are watching, but as servants of Christ, doing the will of God from your heart.
Serve with good will, as to the Lord and not to men,
because you know that the Lord will reward each one for whatever good he does, whether he is slave or free.

And masters, do the same for your slaves. Give up your use of threats, because you know that He who is both their {{kyrios|Master}} and yours is in heaven, and there is no {{prosopolempsia|favoritism}} with Him.

hupakouo.gloss: To obey, literally to listen under, to hear and heed with a readiness to comply. It is a stronger and more active word than mere submission; it is the glad heeding of one set in authority. Children are called to obey their parents in the Lord, and slaves to obey their masters as they would obey Christ. In both cases the obedience is offered within the sphere of Christ's lordship and directed ultimately to Him.

entole.gloss: A commandment, an authoritative charge or instruction. Paul cites the fifth commandment, honor your father and mother, and notes it is the first commandment carrying an attached promise. The word roots the everyday duty of a child in the abiding moral law of God given at Sinai, showing that the household order Paul teaches is not new invention but the outworking of God's long-standing will.

paideia.gloss: Training, discipline, upbringing, the whole formation of a child through instruction and correction. It is not mere punishment but the patient nurturing of a child toward maturity. Fathers are charged to bring their children up in the paideia of the Lord, a formation shaped by and aimed at Christ, the opposite of the harshness that would provoke a child to wrath.

doulos.gloss: A slave or bondservant, one wholly bound to the service of another. In the Roman world such persons had no legal standing, yet Paul addresses them directly as responsible members of the church and as servants of Christ. The same word he uses of the bondslave he uses elsewhere of himself as a servant of Christ, so that the lowest station in the household is dignified as a place of true service rendered to the Lord.

kyrios.gloss: Lord, master, one who holds authority and ownership. Paul uses the word with deliberate force: the earthly master is a kyrios, but he himself has a kyrios in heaven, the same Master over slave and free alike. The word that names the master's power over his slave also names the greater Lord to whom the master must answer, leveling the ground before God and undercutting every abuse of earthly authority.

prosopolempsia.gloss: Partiality, favoritism, literally the receiving of a face, judging by outward appearance or status rather than by truth. God shows no such partiality: He judges slave and free, master and servant, by the same measure. This divine impartiality is the ground on which Paul relativizes the master-slave relationship, for the God who owns them both plays no favorites and will reward each according to the good he has done.

basic.context: Paul keeps applying the Spirit-filled life to the home. Children are to obey and honor their parents, and Paul reminds them that the command to honor father and mother is the first with a promise attached. Fathers are not to exasperate their children but to raise them in the discipline and instruction of the Lord. Then Paul turns to slaves and masters. Slaves are to serve sincerely, as though serving Christ Himself, knowing the Lord will reward them. Masters are to treat their slaves the same way and give up threats, because they share the same Master in heaven, who shows no favoritism.
basic.hermeneutics: The passage falls into two pairs, children and parents, then slaves and masters, each addressing first the one under authority and then the one in authority. Throughout, every relationship is reframed by the phrase in the Lord and by the reminder of a heavenly Master. The abiding principle is not the preservation of ancient social structures but the transformation of every relationship by accountability to Christ.
basic.originalLanguages: Children are to hupakouo, to obey, to listen under, their parents, for the fifth entole, commandment, carries a promise. Fathers are to raise them in the paideia, the discipline and formation, of the Lord. The doulos, the slave, is to serve as a servant of Christ, and the master is reminded that he too has a kyrios, a Master, in heaven, with whom there is no prosopolempsia, no favoritism.
basic.typology: The honoring of father and mother reaches back to the law of Sinai and forward to Christ, the true Son who perfectly honored His Father. The service of the bondslave, rendered as to the Lord, foreshadows the truth that all believers are servants of Christ who was Himself the servant of all, taking the form of a doulos.
basic.christ: Christ is Lord over the whole household. Children obey in Him, fathers nurture in Him, slaves serve as though serving Him, and masters answer to Him. He is the Master in heaven who has no favorites and who rewards each one for the good he does. The passage assumes that every human authority is answerable to the one Lord who took the form of a servant.
basic.god: God is the giver of the commandment to honor parents and the guarantor of its promise. He is the impartial Judge who shows no favoritism, weighing slave and free by the same measure, and the heavenly Master over every earthly master. The passage reveals a God whose justice reaches into every household and levels the proud.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 20 and Deuteronomy 5 give the fifth commandment Paul quotes. Colossians 3 gives the parallel instructions to children, fathers, slaves, and masters. Philemon shows Paul reframing a master and slave as brothers in Christ. Philippians 2 says Christ took the form of a servant, the pattern of all Christian service.
basic.oneStory: God's design for the family goes back to creation and the command at Sinai to honor father and mother. In Christ, every relationship is taken up and reordered around His lordship, and the gospel plants a seed that dignifies the lowly and humbles the powerful. The impartial Master in heaven who levels slave and free points ahead to the kingdom where the last are first and every faithful servant is rewarded.

context.literary: These verses complete the household code that began at 5:21, the third and fourth of the paired relationships after husbands and wives. As with the others, Paul addresses the one under authority first, children and slaves, and then the one holding authority, fathers and masters. The whole code hangs on the participle of 5:18, be filled with the Spirit, and its mark in 5:21, submitting to one another out of reverence for Christ.
context.historical: In the Greco-Roman household the father held sweeping authority over both children and slaves, who were regarded as property with little legal standing. Paul's code is remarkable for addressing children and slaves directly as moral agents and members of the church, for restraining the father from provoking his children, and for placing the master under the same heavenly Lord as his slave. Slavery was woven through the ancient economy, and the Ephesian church included both slaves and masters worshiping side by side.
context.canonical: The passage stands beside Colossians 3:18 to 4:1, 1 Peter 2:18-25, and 1 Timothy 6:1-2 as the New Testament's household teaching, and beside Philemon as a case study in how the gospel reframes master and slave as brothers. Its citation of the fifth commandment ties it to the Decalogue of Exodus 20 and Deuteronomy 5, and its vision of an impartial God links it to Deuteronomy 10:17, Acts 10:34, and Romans 2:11.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To bring children, parents, slaves, and masters alike under the lordship of Christ, calling each to fulfill their place not by mere social convention but as service rendered to the Lord, and to plant in the household the gospel truth that slave and free stand equal before an impartial heavenly Master.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The commands to obey, to honor, to nurture, and to deal justly are prescriptive for the Christian household. The address to slaves and masters describes and regulates an existing institution rather than endorsing it; Paul writes into a world where slavery was universal, and he plants the gospel principles, common Master, no favoritism, that would in time undermine the institution itself. The abiding application is that all our work and all our authority are exercised before Christ.

debates.question: How does the household code on slavery relate to the institution of slavery itself?

views.label: Paul regulates and radically relativizes, rather than endorses, slavery
views.case: Paul does not command the founding or defense of slavery; he addresses believers already caught within a universal institution and reorders the relationship from within. He dignifies the slave as a servant of Christ whose good deeds the Lord will reward, whether slave or free. He restrains the master, telling him to give up threats and to treat his slaves the same way, and he grounds this in a startling equality: the master and the slave share one Master in heaven, with whom there is no favoritism. Read alongside Philemon, where Paul sends a runaway slave back as a beloved brother, and Galatians 3:28, where in Christ there is neither slave nor free, this teaching plants a gospel seed that cannot finally coexist with owning another person made in God's image.

views.label: The gospel principle here worked over time to undermine slavery
views.case: The New Testament does not call for immediate, violent abolition in a world where such a revolt would have been crushed and the church destroyed, but it lays down principles that hollow out slavery's foundations: the equal dignity of slave and master before God, the accountability of every master to a heavenly Lord, and the impartiality of divine judgment. Where these truths were taken seriously, the ground beneath the institution gave way, and Christians came in later centuries to see slavery as incompatible with the gospel. The passage should be read as the seed of that later fruit, not as a permanent endorsement.

debates.bearing: Care and orthodoxy require that we neither read Paul anachronistically, as if he wrote an abolitionist manifesto, nor twist him into a defender of slavery, which he never is. His concern is to bring both slave and master under Christ. Applied today, the passage speaks most directly to the relationship of worker and employer: it calls the employee to sincere, wholehearted work done as unto the Lord rather than mere eye-service, and the one in authority to justice, to the renouncing of intimidation, and to the constant remembrance that he too answers to a Master in heaven who shows no favoritism. The heart of the passage is that all labor and all authority are exercised before Christ.

study.originalLanguages: Children are to hupakouo, to obey, literally to listen under, their parents in the Lord, and Paul grounds this in the fifth entole, commandment, which he notes is the first to carry a promise. Fathers are charged not to provoke but to raise their children in the paideia of the Lord, the patient training and formation that shapes a child toward Christ rather than the harshness that embitters. Then the doulos, the slave, is addressed as a servant of Christ, called to serve from the heart as to the Lord. And the master is reminded that he shares a kyrios, a Master, in heaven, with whom there is no prosopolempsia, no favoritism, no receiving of faces, for God weighs slave and free by the same measure.

typology.fulfillment: The fifth commandment is embodied perfectly in Christ, the Son who honored His Father unto death, and it is written on the hearts of His people by the Spirit.

typology.type: The bondslave who serves as to the Lord
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ who took the very form of a doulos, a servant, and in every believer who is now a servant of Christ, the lowest station made a place of honored service.
Points forward to the judgment seat of Christ, where the Lord who shows no favoritism will reward each one for the good he has done, slave or free.

study.christ: Christ stands over the whole household as its true Lord. Children obey their parents in Him; fathers raise their children in His discipline and instruction; slaves serve their earthly masters as though serving Him, doing the will of God from the heart; and masters answer to Him as the Master in heaven who is over both them and their slaves. He is the impartial Lord who will reward each one for whatever good he does, without favoritism. And He is Himself the servant who took the form of a bondslave, so that the service He asks of the lowliest He first rendered in love. Every relationship in the passage is measured by His lordship and His example.
study.god: God is the lawgiver who commanded children to honor their parents and attached to it His own promise of blessing. He is the impartial Judge, the God who shows no favoritism, weighing master and slave, powerful and powerless, by one measure. And He is the heavenly Master over every earthly authority, before whom the proud are leveled and the faithful are rewarded. The passage reveals a God whose justice pervades the most ordinary corners of daily life and who will not be mocked by the abuse of power.

crossReferences.note: The fifth commandment, honor your father and mother, with its promise, which Paul quotes in verses 2 and 3.
The parallel household code addressing children, fathers, slaves, and masters, with the same appeal to a Master in heaven.
Paul sends the runaway slave Onesimus back to his master no longer as a slave but as a beloved brother, the gospel reframing the relationship.
Christ took the form of a servant, a doulos, the pattern of the service Paul commands and the dignity He gives it.

study.oneStory: God's care for the household runs from the command at Sinai to honor father and mother through to the reordering of every relationship in Christ. Into a world of rigid hierarchy the gospel comes and plants a seed: children and slaves are addressed as persons of worth, masters are humbled before a heavenly Lord, and slave and free are declared equal in the sight of the God who shows no favoritism. That seed, growing across the centuries, would help unmake slavery itself, and it points ahead to the kingdom where the servant of all is Lord of all and every faithful deed is remembered and rewarded.

quiz.q: Why does Paul say the command to honor father and mother is notable?

options.text: It is the first commandment with a promise attached, that it may go well and life be long.
options.note: Verses 2 and 3 highlight the promise joined to the fifth commandment.

options.text: It is the only commandment that still applies to Christians.
options.note: Paul singles out its attached promise, not that it is the sole surviving command.

options.text: It was a new rule Paul invented for the church.
options.note: Paul quotes the ancient fifth commandment from the law, not a new invention.

options.text: It applies only to adult children, never young ones.
options.note: Paul addresses children directly to obey and honor their parents.

quiz.q: What are fathers charged to do and not to do in verse 4?

options.text: Not to provoke their children to wrath, but to raise them in the discipline and instruction of the Lord.
options.note: Fathers are to nurture, not exasperate, forming their children in the Lord.

options.text: To rule their children by fear so they never step out of line.
options.note: Paul warns against provoking to wrath; the aim is godly nurture, not intimidation.

options.text: To leave all instruction to others and stay uninvolved.
options.note: Fathers are actively charged to bring their children up in the Lord.

options.text: To punish every fault as harshly as possible.
options.note: Harshness that provokes children to wrath is exactly what Paul forbids.

quiz.q: How are slaves told to serve their earthly masters?

options.text: Sincerely from the heart, as servants of Christ doing the will of God, not merely to be seen.
options.note: Verses 5 to 7 call for wholehearted service rendered as to the Lord.

options.text: Only when the master is watching, to avoid punishment.
options.note: Paul says the opposite, not to please them only while they watch but as servants of Christ.

options.text: Grudgingly, since the work has no meaning.
options.note: They are to serve with good will, as to the Lord, because He will reward the good they do.

options.text: By refusing to work until they are freed.
options.note: Paul calls for faithful service within the situation, done as unto Christ.

quiz.q: What does Paul remind masters of in verse 9?

options.text: That they share one Master in heaven with their slaves, and there is no favoritism with Him.
options.note: The master answers to the same impartial heavenly Lord as his slave.

options.text: That their authority over slaves is absolute and unquestioned.
options.note: Paul limits their authority, telling them to give up threats and remember their own Master.

options.text: That God favors masters over slaves.
options.note: Paul says exactly the opposite, there is no favoritism with God.

options.text: That they owe their slaves nothing at all.
options.note: Masters are to do the same for their slaves and give up threats, answerable to God.

quiz.q: How does the passage relate to slavery as an institution?

options.text: It regulates and radically relativizes it, planting gospel principles that undermine it over time.
options.note: Common Master and no favoritism level slave and free and hollow out the institution's foundation.

options.text: It gives slavery God's permanent endorsement.
options.note: Paul never endorses slavery; he reorders the relationship under Christ and dignifies the slave.

options.text: It commands an immediate armed revolt against all masters.
options.note: Paul plants transforming principles rather than calling for a violent revolt that would have been crushed.

options.text: It says nothing of any relevance to how we work today.
options.note: The principles apply directly to modern work: sincere labor and just, humble authority, all done before Christ.

quiz.q: What truth grounds both the slave's service and the master's restraint?

options.text: That the Lord in heaven rewards each one for the good he does and shows no favoritism.
options.note: The impartial heavenly Master is the ground for both wholehearted service and just authority.

options.text: That earthly status determines one's worth before God.
options.note: Paul teaches the reverse: God shows no favoritism between slave and free.

options.text: That good deeds go unnoticed and unrewarded.
options.note: Paul says the Lord will reward each one for whatever good he does.

options.text: That masters answer to no one for their conduct.
options.note: Masters answer to the same Master in heaven who is over them and their slaves.

questions: Paul tells children to obey and honor their parents in the Lord. However old you are, how might you honor your father and mother in a way that reflects your reverence for Christ?
Fathers are warned not to provoke their children to wrath but to nurture them in the Lord. Where might firmness need to be tempered by patience in the way you shape those under your care?
Slaves were told to work sincerely as servants of Christ, not merely to be seen. How would treating your daily work as service rendered to the Lord change the way you do it?
Masters were told to give up threats and to remember their own Master in heaven. If you hold authority over anyone, how does knowing you answer to an impartial God shape how you use it?
God shows no favoritism, leveling slave and free before Him. How does the impartial justice of God comfort you if you feel powerless, and humble you if you hold power?

translationNotes.note: These verses quote the fifth commandment from Exodus 20:12 and Deuteronomy 5:16, honor your father and mother, with its attached promise; the straight double quotation marks preserve the citation of the Decalogue.
Discipline renders paideia, the whole training and formation of a child through instruction and correction, not mere punishment; the phrase of the Lord marks it as a Christ-shaped upbringing.
Favoritism renders prosopolempsia, literally the receiving of a face, that is, judging by status or appearance; Paul's point is that God weighs master and slave by one impartial measure.

meta.notes: BSB text for Ephesians 6:1-9 reproduced as supplied. Verses 2 and 3 quote Exodus 20:12 and Deuteronomy 5:16, kept with straight double quotes. No em or en dashes used; punctuation kept clean per the style rule.
